region_of_origin: South America (cassava origin); widely used across Southeast Asia, South Asia, and globally

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a tapioca food product entry; tapioca flour / starch is the closest defensible match. No dedicated tapioca flour URI confirmed; 'closest_parent' match type used as it represents a broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No confirmed SNOMED CT code for tapioca flour as a food ingredient; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of tapioca flour used as a thickener or binder is approximately 1 tablespoon (12 g), contributing about 42 kcal and 10.5 g of carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Tapioca, pearl, dry — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169717); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for Tapioca flour (cr_id F05040013)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore primary source for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron as provided in input record (confidence 0.95). USDA SR Legacy used to supplement sodium, sugar, saturated fat, and additional micronutrients for tapioca starch (FDC ID 169717). Values are consistent between sources. HPB values preserved where available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Tapioca (boiled with milk) GI ~81; tapioca starch analogous estimate GI ~70 per University of Sydney GI Database entry for tapioca starch.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Tapioca starch GI is reported at approximately 70 (high) in the University of Sydney GI Database. The Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care tables report tapioca boiled with milk at GI 81. A conservative estimate of GI 70 is used for dry tapioca flour/starch as a raw ingredient, consistent with published values for refined cassava starch. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (70 × 10.49) / 100 ≈ 7.4 for a 12 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content (~1 mg/100 g); no added salt in raw flour
hypertension.guidance: Tapioca flour is very low in sodium (approximately 1 mg/100 g) and does not trigger any hypertension hard gates as a raw ingredient; however, the sodium content of any prepared dish depends entirely on other ingredients added during cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high refined carbohydrate content (87.4 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g) and high glycaemic index (~70), causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit tapioca flour to very small quantities (5–10 g) used only as a thickener, as its high GI (~70) and near-absence of fibre produce rapid blood glucose spikes; lower-GI alternatives such as almond flour or coconut flour are strongly preferred for baking.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined starch with high glycaemic index contributes to elevated postprandial triglycerides and insulin resistance when consumed in large quantities; fat and saturated fat content are negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tapioca flour is virtually fat-free and sodium-free, posing no direct lipid or blood-pressure risk, but its high glycaemic index and refined-starch profile can raise postprandial triglycerides and contribute to insulin resistance if used as a primary carbohydrate source; use should be restricted to small thickening quantities within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load with minimal fibre and no anti-inflammatory nutrients; high glycaemic index may promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Tapioca flour provides no anti-inflammatory benefit (no omega-3, polyphenols, or meaningful fibre) and its high glycaemic index may exacerbate inflammatory glycaemic load relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; use only in minimal quantities as a thickener and prefer whole-grain or nut-based flours.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tapioca flour (also called tapioca starch or cassava starch) is a fine, white, odourless powder extracted from the starchy root of the cassava plant (Manihot esculenta), native to South America but now cultivated extensively across Southeast Asia, Africa, and South Asia. The production process involves washing, grating, and pressing the cassava root to extract the starch, which is then dried and milled into a smooth, silky powder. Tapioca flour is almost entirely composed of refined starch (approximately 87.4 g carbohydrate per 100 g) with negligible protein (0.11 g), minimal fat (0.09 g), and low dietary fibre (1.2 g). It is naturally gluten-free, making it a popular wheat-flour substitute in gluten-free baking, though its nutritional profile is far less favourable than whole-grain alternatives. In culinary use, tapioca flour functions primarily as a thickener for soups, sauces, and gravies, as a binder in dumplings and boba pearls, and as a coating for fried foods to achieve a crispy texture. It is a key ingredient in Southeast Asian dishes such as kueh, onde-onde, and various Singaporean and Malaysian kuih. Its high glycaemic index (approximately 70) means it causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation, making it unsuitable as a primary carbohydrate source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. Clinically, tapioca flour is best used in small quantities (5–10 g) as a functional ingredient rather than a nutritional staple. Calcium content is notably higher than expected for a refined starch (84 mg/100 g per HPB data), which may reflect mineral retention during processing.

meal_context: Thickening agent in soups and gravies

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95, reviewed status). Secondary micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169717) for tapioca starch, which is consistent with HPB values. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication.
notes: Protein_g (0.11) and fat_g (0.09) are very low but non-zero trace amounts confirmed by HPB Singapore database; these are not zero values and no zero-macro justification is required. Trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/absent in refined cassava starch per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: plant-derived starch contains no cholesterol — naturally zero. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: confirmed negligible or absent in refined tapioca starch per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 169717. Calcium value of 84 mg/100 g is notably elevated for a refined starch; this value is preserved from HPB Singapore source (confidence 0.95) and may reflect mineral retention during cassava processing or analytical methodology differences. USDA SR Legacy reports calcium at approximately 20 mg/100 g for tapioca pearl; the HPB value is retained as the primary regional source. Iron value of 0.9 mg/100 g is similarly higher than USDA SR Legacy (~1.58 mg/100 g for tapioca pearl); HPB value retained. FoodOn URI used is approximate — no dedicated tapioca flour URI was confirmed in FoodOn as of April 2026; the tapioca food product entry is the closest defensible match. FoodEx2 code A00LC for tapioca starch is used as the primary ontology anchor.
